title: Trees & BSTs
description: Hierarchical data structures in Python: Binary Trees, Binary Search Trees (BST), all operations, and depth-first traversals.

sections.title: Binary Tree Traversals
sections.description: Traversing a tree means visiting all nodes. Depth-First search traversals include Preorder (Root-Left-Right), Inorder (Left-Root-Right), and Postorder (Left-Right-Root), all executing in O(N) time.
sections.codeBlock:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

# Construct tree
root = TreeNode(1)
root.left = TreeNode(2)
root.right = TreeNode(3)

# 1. Preorder - O(N)
def preorder(n, res):
    if n:
        res.append(n.val)
        preorder(n.left, res)
        preorder(n.right, res)

# 2. Inorder - O(N)
def inorder(n, res):
    if n:
        inorder(n.left, res)
        res.append(n.val)
        inorder(n.right, res)

# 3. Postorder - O(N)
def postorder(n, res):
    if n:
        postorder(n.left, res)
        postorder(n.right, res)
        res.append(n.val)

pre_path = []
preorder(root, pre_path)
print(pre_path)        # [1, 2, 3] (Root -> Left -> Right)

sections.title: Binary Search Tree Operations
sections.description: Binary Search Trees (BST) restrict node ordering to optimize searching. Key operations include Search, Insert, and Delete. Average time complexities are O(log N), while worst-case is O(N) if the tree is unbalanced.

details:
Tree Traversals: Depth-First traversals visit tree nodes recursively. Preorder (Root-Left-Right) is commonly used to create copies of trees or evaluate prefix mathematical expressions. Inorder (Left-Root-Right) is used on BSTs to retrieve all keys in ascending order. Postorder (Left-Right-Root) is useful for post-fix expressions and deleting nodes (bottom-up execution ensures child nodes are deleted before parent nodes are cleaned).

BST Complexity: Average time complexities for search, insertion, and deletion are O(log N). However, if elements are inserted in sorted order, the tree degenerates into a linear chain (like a linked list), dropping execution speeds to O(N). Balancing structures (such as AVL trees or Red-Black trees) resolve this by self-balancing during insertions.
